The Executive Challenge

Organizations rarely struggle because they lack vision.

They struggle because execution becomes fragmented, strategy stalls in the handoff to operations, and no one owns the system that connects the two.

01

Strategy isn't translating into results.

02

AI initiatives are disconnected from operations.

03

Leaders are working hard, but not together.

04

Shared services aren't delivering enterprise value.

05

Performance metrics don't drive accountability.

06

Growth has outpaced operating discipline.

The Dominguez Operating Framework

Not an optimizer. A builder.

A repeatable, seven-stage methodology, built from twenty-five years of running operations, compensation, and workforce systems inside organizations under real pressure to perform.

Measurable Performance
Every stage of the framework strengthens a layer of the organization.
01
Define Success
"What does success actually look like?"
MCI WorldCom · daily sales MIS built around a clear definition of outcome
02
Measure What Matters
"Are we measuring activity, or outcomes?"
Citigroup · performance gaps analyzed location by location, not team by team
03
Diagnose Constraints
"Where is execution actually breaking down?"
State of New Mexico · 68 agencies, one time-to-fill bottleneck isolated
04
Design Solutions
"Build the operating model, not another initiative."
Citigroup · the 21-Day Path to Success program
05
Pilot & Validate
"Prove it before you scale it."
Citigroup · validated through the 2008 financial crisis, not a calm quarter
06
Scale What Works
"Expand what actually delivers results."
Citigroup · recognition platform scaled to 200,000-plus employees
07
Build Organizational Capability
"Leave the organization stronger than when you arrived."
Leaders retire. CEOs move on. The system should keep performing
Proof

Not testimonials. Evidence.

Four organizations, four different kinds of pressure: a global bank in crisis, a state government under public scrutiny, a telecom scaling fast, and a SaaS company redefining its category.

Citigroup · 2003–2021

Holding performance steady through a financial crisis

Challenge

Global incentive compensation and recognition programs had to keep driving performance through the 2008 downturn, across a 119-country footprint, without losing trust.

Approach

Diagnosed performance gaps by location, redesigned incentive structures, and led UAT for a global HR system rollout spanning 119 countries.

80%incentive plan participation, with a 30-point outperformance versus market during the 2008 crisis
State of New Mexico · 2024–2026

Fixing a hiring bottleneck across 68 agencies

Challenge

Roughly 22,000 employees across 68 state agencies, with time-to-fill dragging on critical roles, plus a $985M early-childhood budget needing operational discipline.

Approach

Rebuilt the hiring pipeline end to end and led the "State Jobs Are Great Jobs" applicant-marketing campaign backed by a $100K Tourism Department investment.

62→45days to fill, alongside an 11.5% rise in applications and an 18.5% rise in applications per posting
MCI WorldCom · 1997–2003

Giving sales leadership a real-time picture

Challenge

Sales performance was visible only after the fact, too late for leaders to adjust course mid-cycle.

Approach

Built a daily sales management information system, turning lagging reports into a near-real-time forecasting tool.

Outcome

The system became the foundation for the analytical, hypothesis-driven approach carried into every organization since.

Icertis · 2022–2023

Bringing performance discipline into fast-scaling SaaS

Challenge

A fast-growing contract intelligence company needed enterprise-grade people practices without slowing down its pace.

Approach

Applied the same operating discipline built at global institutions to a product-and-customer-facing organization moving at startup speed.

Outcome

Proof that the framework travels: it holds up in a 100-year-old bank and in a company still writing its playbook.

How I Think

The questions that shape the work.

01
Why do transformations fail?
Almost never for lack of a good plan. They fail in the handoff, when strategy reaches the operating layer and there is no system built to carry it. The plan gets a launch event; the execution gets nothing. My approach starts by treating execution itself as something you design, measure, and pilot, not something you simply hope for after the strategy deck is approved.
02
What does operational excellence really mean?
It means the organization performs the same way whether or not a specific leader is in the room. Excellence that depends on one person's memory or relationships is fragile. Real operational excellence is written down, measured, and repeatable, so performance survives leadership transitions instead of resetting with every new hire.
03
How should executives approach AI?
Not as a separate initiative with its own budget line and steering committee, but as an operating capability woven into diagnosis, workflow design, coaching, and decision support. The organizations that get real value treat AI the way they treat any other operating system component: something to pilot, measure, and scale, not something to announce.
04
What makes leaders effective?
Leaders are effective when they build capability in the people and systems around them, not just in themselves. The measure of a leader isn't what happens while they're in the role; it's what keeps working after they've left it.
05
Why do operating models matter?
Because a strategy without an operating model is just an intention. The operating model is where strategy meets accountability: who owns which decision, what gets measured, and how the organization corrects course when reality doesn't match the plan. Get the operating model right, and strategy has somewhere real to land.
Executive Insights

Not a blog. Working thinking.

Operating Model

Why Organizations Don't Execute Strategy

At Citigroup, the strategy was never the problem; the gap showed up location by location, once performance was actually measured against it. That diagnosis, not a new strategy deck, is what led to the 21-Day Path to Success program.

Shared Services

The Future of Shared Services

Running HR and hiring operations across 68 state agencies and roughly 22,000 employees only works if the shared service is designed as one system, not 68 separate relationships. The fix was operational, not political.

AI & Operations

AI Will Change Operations Before It Changes Jobs

The tools that moved the needle first weren't strategic AI initiatives; they were AI folded into daily workflow: SOP creation, applicant tooling, coaching support. Capability changed before any job description did.

Performance

What Every CEO Should Measure

Time-to-fill, participation rate, market outperformance: none of these matter in isolation. What matters is locking a small set of proof-point metrics and holding every initiative accountable to the same numbers, quarter after quarter.

Scale

Designing Organizations That Scale

A recognition platform reaching 200,000-plus employees across a 119-country footprint doesn't scale by accident. It scales because the operating model was validated small, under real pressure, before it was ever rolled out wide.

Complexity

The Hidden Cost of Organizational Complexity

Layer enough regulatory frameworks onto an organization, whether that's SOX and GDPR or state administrative law and a $985M public budget, and complexity itself becomes the risk. Compliance mapping has to be treated as an operating discipline, not paperwork.
